Here we analyse seamount catalogues and show that seamount volcanism is more voluminous on seafloor that has passed above a LLSVP. We find that this volcanic enhancement is evident above both LLSVPs, but is greater above the Pacific one and has declined since the mid-Cretaceous. We suggest that weak plumes rising from both LLSVPs interact with oceanic lithosphere to promote seamount volcanism. This process may be enhanced for the Pacific because of elevated mantle temperature, transition zone hydration and/or convective vigour in the Pacific hemisphere. Our findings provide additional support for the hypothesis that plumes carrying heat from the LLSVPs are responsible for most oceanic intraplate volcanism, including dispersed seamounts. This deep mantle connection means that seamount volcanism is sensitive to, and thus may constrain, the time-dependent dynamics of Earth’s deepest mantle. Seamount volcanism is more voluminous where seafloor has passed above a large low-shear-velocity province, consistent with mantle plumes being responsible for most oceanic intraplate volcanism, according to an analysis of seamount catalogues.

item.title: Triassic aeolian megadune field reveals paleo-monsoon wind directions for low-latitude Gondwana
item.dc:creator: Rossano D. L. Michel
Claiton M. S. Scherer
Nigel P. Mountney
Adam D. McArthur
Adriano D. Reis
Jairo F. Savian
Carrel Kifumbi
Na Yan
Ronaldo P. Cechetti
Luca Colombera
item.description: Abstract Linear dunes are rarely recognized in the geological record. By integrating sedimentological observations with aeromagnetic, magnetic susceptibility and magnetization datasets, we reconstruct the morphology of an Upper Triassic aeolian dune field from the sedimentary architecture of deposits preserved in the Sambaíba Formation, Parnaíba Basin, from northeast Brazil. This dune field was buried by lavas from the central Atlantic magmatic province. Aeolian architecture records the migration of superimposed crescentic dunes along the flanks of parent linear megadunes. These bedforms were part of a large aeolian dune field (>55,000 km 2 ), comprising megadunes with east-northeast to west-southwest oriented crestlines, up to 167 m in height, and ~2.7 km in crest-to-crest spacing. Mantled by lava, it is the largest and best-preserved instance of a linear megadune field yet documented in the rock record. The dune field developed in response to the coalescence of north-easterly and south-easterly paleowinds driven by a monsoon system operating at low latitudes (~10°S) of Gondwana during the Late Triassic: this validates atmospheric circulation models which had lacked direct geological support. Published: 2026-06-10T08:12:47+00:00 Venue: Communications Earth & Environment Score: 0.445 (flagship)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rossano D. L. Michel; Claiton M. S. Scherer; Nigel P. Mountney; Adam D. McArthur; Adriano D. Reis; Jairo F. Savian; Carrel Kifumbi; Na Yan; Ronaldo P. Cechetti; Luca Colombera

Journal: Communications Earth & Environment

DOI: 10.1038/s43247-026-03697-4

Relevance: 0.445 (flagship)

Abstract Linear dunes are rarely recognized in the geological record. By integrating sedimentological observations with aeromagnetic, magnetic susceptibility and magnetization datasets, we reconstruct the morphology of an Upper Triassic aeolian dune field from the sedimentary architecture of deposits preserved in the Sambaíba Formation, Parnaíba Basin, from northeast Brazil. This dune field was buried by lavas from the central Atlantic magmatic province. Aeolian architecture records the migration of superimposed crescentic dunes along the flanks of parent linear megadunes. These bedforms were part of a large aeolian dune field (>55,000 km 2 ), comprising megadunes with east-northeast to west-southwest oriented crestlines, up to 167 m in height, and ~2.7 km in crest-to-crest spacing. Mantled by lava, it is the largest and best-preserved instance of a linear megadune field yet documented in the rock record. The dune field developed in response to the coalescence of north-easterly and south-easterly paleowinds driven by a monsoon system operating at low latitudes (~10°S) of Gondwana during the Late Triassic: this validates atmospheric circulation models which had lacked direct geological support.

item.title: Deep mantle reservoirs of recycled surface carbonates revealed by Mg-Sr-Nd-Pb isotopes in continental basalts
item.dc:creator: Yan-Qing Li
Chang-Qian Ma
Xiao-Ping Xia
Bin Liu
Ji-Ping Yu
Xiang-Yun Hu
item.description: Deep carbon recycling plays a pivotal role in redistributing carbon among the Earth’s internal reservoirs and modulating global climate over geological timescales. However, the depth at which subducted carbon is stored in the deep mantle, and the dynamics of its recycling remain poorly constrained. Subducted carbonates, typically characterized by lower δ26Mg values relative to the ambient mantle, make magnesium isotopes a powerful tracer for identifying recycled carbonates in mantle-derived rocks. In Eastern China, widespread Cenozoic basaltic volcanism is thought to be driven by hot and/or hydrous upwellings originating from the mantle transition zone (MTZ). Here, we present new Mg isotope data for Cenozoic basalts from Southeast China. Through a comprehensive comparison of Mg-Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic and elemental compositions of basalts from both Northeast and Southeast China, we identify distinct mechanisms of deep carbon recycling beneath the two regions. The low δ26Mg values observed in the Northeast China basalts are attributed to contributions from eclogites metamorphosed predominantly from ancient carbonated sediments stored within the MTZ. In contrast, the recycled carbon signatures in Southeast China basalts were derived mainly from eclogites metamorphosed from dehydrated oceanic crust, with only minor contributions from recycled sediments stored in the MTZ and possibly the deeper mantle. Considering the contrasting tectonic evolutions of Northeast and Southeast China since the Mesozoic, we propose a dynamic link between western Pacific Plate subduction and regionally distinct deep carbon cycling processes. Continuous subduction of the western Pacific Plate has formed a hydrous, carbonate-rich mantle domain beneath Northeast China, where fluid-induced partial melting acts as the key mechanism for carbon recycling. Conversely, dense, dehydrated carbonated eclogite fragments preserved in the deep mantle beneath Southeast China are difficult to ascend on their own, with local thermal upwellings providing the necessary dynamics for magma ascent. Our findings highlight that mantle hydration critically influences the efficiency of deep carbon recycling back to the surface through volcanism, with broader implications for the long-term stability of Earth’s carbon cycle Published: 2026-06-11T19:24:38+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.736 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan-Qing Li; Chang-Qian Ma; Xiao-Ping Xia; Bin Liu; Ji-Ping Yu; Xiang-Yun Hu

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.06.010

Relevance: 0.736 (must_read)

Deep carbon recycling plays a pivotal role in redistributing carbon among the Earth’s internal reservoirs and modulating global climate over geological timescales. However, the depth at which subducted carbon is stored in the deep mantle, and the dynamics of its recycling remain poorly constrained. Subducted carbonates, typically characterized by lower δ26Mg values relative to the ambient mantle, make magnesium isotopes a powerful tracer for identifying recycled carbonates in mantle-derived rocks. In Eastern China, widespread Cenozoic basaltic volcanism is thought to be driven by hot and/or hydrous upwellings originating from the mantle transition zone (MTZ). Here, we present new Mg isotope data for Cenozoic basalts from Southeast China. Through a comprehensive comparison of Mg-Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic and elemental compositions of basalts from both Northeast and Southeast China, we identify distinct mechanisms of deep carbon recycling beneath the two regions. The low δ26Mg values observed in the Northeast China basalts are attributed to contributions from eclogites metamorphosed predominantly from ancient carbonated sediments stored within the MTZ. In contrast, the recycled carbon signatures in Southeast China basalts were derived mainly from eclogites metamorphosed from dehydrated oceanic crust, with only minor contributions from recycled sediments stored in the MTZ and possibly the deeper mantle. Considering the contrasting tectonic evolutions of Northeast and Southeast China since the Mesozoic, we propose a dynamic link between western Pacific Plate subduction and regionally distinct deep carbon cycling processes. Continuous subduction of the western Pacific Plate has formed a hydrous, carbonate-rich mantle domain beneath Northeast China, where fluid-induced partial melting acts as the key mechanism for carbon recycling. Conversely, dense, dehydrated carbonated eclogite fragments preserved in the deep mantle beneath Southeast China are difficult to ascend on their own, with local thermal upwellings providing the necessary dynamics for magma ascent. Our findings highlight that mantle hydration critically influences the efficiency of deep carbon recycling back to the surface through volcanism, with broader implications for the long-term stability of Earth’s carbon cycle

item.title: Prediction of sulfur-iron redox systematics during mantle wedge metasomatism by slab fluids
item.dc:creator: Patrick Beaudry
Dimitri A. Sverjensky
item.description: Chemical mass transfer in subduction zones is a fundamental aspect of plate tectonics on Earth, driving arc magmatism and the recycling of subducted materials to the surface. The reactive mechanisms between aqueous fluids released by slab dehydration and mantle minerals are not well understood, but are likely associated with the transfer of redox potential, which may explain the generally oxidized character of arc magmas. Here we use the Deep Earth Water (DEW) model to perform mass transfer calculations of sulfur-saturated slab fluids at variably oxidized conditions (FMQ + 1 to FMQ + 2) reacting with garnet lherzolite assemblages. Fe3+-bearing multi-component phases are considered in DEW calculations for the first time, including Fe-Ti-Cr-Mg-Al oxides (ilmenite and spinel solid solutions), as well as clinopyroxene (acmite, NaFe3+Si2O6; esseneite, CaFe3+AlSiO6) and garnet (andradite, Ca3Fe3+ 2Si3O12) components. Fluid-rock reaction heating paths are calculated at fixed pressure (2 and 3 GPa) and temperature increasing from 650 to 950°C. At those subarc conditions, sulfate is very efficient at oxidizing iron and producing overall oxidized mineral products after reaction with the depleted mantle, whereas CO2-rich fluids cause only marginal oxidation. Modeled Fe2O3 contents in spinel, clinopyroxene and garnet match those of natural data from variably metasomatized xenoliths. Modeled bulk rock Fe2O3 contents are also similar to those of oxidized arc xenoliths. Furthermore, despite oxidizing significant quantities of mantle iron, predicted fluids remain sulfate-rich owing to very high initial sulfur solubilities. This implies that mantle metasomatism with sulfur-rich, oxidized slab fluids could lead to arc magmas with sulfur budgets and isotopic compositions dominated by recycled subducted sulfur. We further show that our predicted concentrations of sulfur in slab fluids are generally consistent with the global fluxes of water and sulfur in and out of subduction zones. Published: 2026-06-10T23:56:02+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.682 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Patrick Beaudry; Dimitri A. Sverjensky

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.06.009

Relevance: 0.682 (must_read)

Chemical mass transfer in subduction zones is a fundamental aspect of plate tectonics on Earth, driving arc magmatism and the recycling of subducted materials to the surface. The reactive mechanisms between aqueous fluids released by slab dehydration and mantle minerals are not well understood, but are likely associated with the transfer of redox potential, which may explain the generally oxidized character of arc magmas. Here we use the Deep Earth Water (DEW) model to perform mass transfer calculations of sulfur-saturated slab fluids at variably oxidized conditions (FMQ + 1 to FMQ + 2) reacting with garnet lherzolite assemblages. Fe3+-bearing multi-component phases are considered in DEW calculations for the first time, including Fe-Ti-Cr-Mg-Al oxides (ilmenite and spinel solid solutions), as well as clinopyroxene (acmite, NaFe3+Si2O6; esseneite, CaFe3+AlSiO6) and garnet (andradite, Ca3Fe3+ 2Si3O12) components. Fluid-rock reaction heating paths are calculated at fixed pressure (2 and 3 GPa) and temperature increasing from 650 to 950°C. At those subarc conditions, sulfate is very efficient at oxidizing iron and producing overall oxidized mineral products after reaction with the depleted mantle, whereas CO2-rich fluids cause only marginal oxidation. Modeled Fe2O3 contents in spinel, clinopyroxene and garnet match those of natural data from variably metasomatized xenoliths. Modeled bulk rock Fe2O3 contents are also similar to those of oxidized arc xenoliths. Furthermore, despite oxidizing significant quantities of mantle iron, predicted fluids remain sulfate-rich owing to very high initial sulfur solubilities. This implies that mantle metasomatism with sulfur-rich, oxidized slab fluids could lead to arc magmas with sulfur budgets and isotopic compositions dominated by recycled subducted sulfur. We further show that our predicted concentrations of sulfur in slab fluids are generally consistent with the global fluxes of water and sulfur in and out of subduction zones.

item.title: Forearc peridotites record interaction of slab melt with nascent mantle wedge in the southwest Mariana arc
item.dc:creator: Junhua Yao
Guo-Liang Zhang
Ji Zhang
De-Feng He
item.description: Plate subduction plays a critical role in plate tectonics, yet the interaction between the subducted plate and the mantle wedge in nascent subduction zones remains poorly understood. The southwestern Mariana arc located in the western Pacific near Micronesia, representing a modern analog of subduction infancy, provides an ideal setting to investigate subduction infancy and the recycling of subducted materials. Here we present petrographic, mineralogical, and whole-rock geochemical data for serpentinized forearc peridotites recovered from the southwest Mariana arc. These forearc peridotites can be divided into two groups based on their chromite Cr# values. The high-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.78−0.86) records interaction with high-Si boninitic melts, as indicated by the presence of chromite-hosted amphibole inclusions whose trace-element compositions are consistent with a high-Si boninitic affinity, whereas the low-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.49−0.74) is interpreted to have experienced metasomatism by melts of adakitic affinity, as suggested by the occurrence of clinopyroxene with adakite-like geochemical characteristics. Compared to the mature Izu-Bonin and central Mariana arcs, the southwestern Mariana forearc peridotites are geochemically more enriched, showing higher SiO2 and Fe2O3T, lower MgO, and up to an order of magnitude higher heavy rare earth element contents. These features indicate that the forearc peridotites in immature subduction zones preserve more pristine records of early mantle wedge processes, owing to limited subsequent modification after the initial stages of subduction. Our findings challenge the prevailing view that forearc peridotites are highly depleted and refractory and highlight the importance of early melt−rock interaction in shaping sub-arc mantle wedge compositions during subduction initiation. Published: 2026-06-16T22:08:09+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.624 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Junhua Yao; Guo-Liang Zhang; Ji Zhang; De-Feng He

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38968.1

Relevance: 0.624 (must_read)

Plate subduction plays a critical role in plate tectonics, yet the interaction between the subducted plate and the mantle wedge in nascent subduction zones remains poorly understood. The southwestern Mariana arc located in the western Pacific near Micronesia, representing a modern analog of subduction infancy, provides an ideal setting to investigate subduction infancy and the recycling of subducted materials. Here we present petrographic, mineralogical, and whole-rock geochemical data for serpentinized forearc peridotites recovered from the southwest Mariana arc. These forearc peridotites can be divided into two groups based on their chromite Cr# values. The high-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.78−0.86) records interaction with high-Si boninitic melts, as indicated by the presence of chromite-hosted amphibole inclusions whose trace-element compositions are consistent with a high-Si boninitic affinity, whereas the low-Cr# group (Cr#: 0.49−0.74) is interpreted to have experienced metasomatism by melts of adakitic affinity, as suggested by the occurrence of clinopyroxene with adakite-like geochemical characteristics. Compared to the mature Izu-Bonin and central Mariana arcs, the southwestern Mariana forearc peridotites are geochemically more enriched, showing higher SiO2 and Fe2O3T, lower MgO, and up to an order of magnitude higher heavy rare earth element contents. These features indicate that the forearc peridotites in immature subduction zones preserve more pristine records of early mantle wedge processes, owing to limited subsequent modification after the initial stages of subduction. Our findings challenge the prevailing view that forearc peridotites are highly depleted and refractory and highlight the importance of early melt−rock interaction in shaping sub-arc mantle wedge compositions during subduction initiation.

item.title: Lithospheric Melting and Gold Mineralization in the Southwestern Cathaysia Block: Constraints From Magnetotelluric Evidence
item.dc:creator: Yufeng Chen
Jiayong Yan
Juzhi Deng
Hui Chen
Yuexin You
Hui Yu
Zhen Deng
Fan Luo
Xu Wang
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract Mesozoic Paleo‐Pacific plate subduction was dominant and drove intense continental reworking in South China, coupled with voluminous magmatism and episodic mineralization events. However, the lack of detailed geophysical constraints on the lithospheric architecture of southwestern Cathaysia Block hinders resolution of its alteration mechanisms and metallogenic evolution. We acquired magnetotelluric array data at 82 sites across southwestern Cathaysia Block, constraining the lithospheric resistivity architecture of this region. Integrating laboratory measurements of mantle minerals, broadband seismic data, and geothermal constraints, we quantitatively constrain the origin of lithospheric mantle conductivity anomalies. Research findings indicate that the crust in the study area is predominantly characterized by a high‐resistivity background, interspersed with linear conductive anomalies spatially correlated with fault zones. The conductive features in the lithospheric mantle likely result from the combined effects of partial melting and water in the mantle. Based on the regional tectonic framework, we propose that water released from previously subducted slabs and associated metasomatism weakened the lithosphere, potentially leading to delamination of the lithospheric root. This process, accompanied by slab collapse, triggered asthenospheric upwelling and thermo‐mechanical perturbations. Thermal erosion by the upwelling asthenosphere facilitated decompression melting within the thinned lithospheric mantle and the overlying, extended crust. The Paleo‐Pacific subduction system provided the necessary conduits for sourcing and transporting Au‐rich fluids, ultimately facilitating gold mineralization in the study area. Published: 2026-06-11T11:15:47+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.621 (must_read)

Authors: Yufeng Chen; Jiayong Yan; Juzhi Deng; Hui Chen; Yuexin You; Hui Yu; Zhen Deng; Fan Luo; Xu Wang

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb032615

Relevance: 0.621 (must_read)

Abstract Mesozoic Paleo‐Pacific plate subduction was dominant and drove intense continental reworking in South China, coupled with voluminous magmatism and episodic mineralization events. However, the lack of detailed geophysical constraints on the lithospheric architecture of southwestern Cathaysia Block hinders resolution of its alteration mechanisms and metallogenic evolution. We acquired magnetotelluric array data at 82 sites across southwestern Cathaysia Block, constraining the lithospheric resistivity architecture of this region. Integrating laboratory measurements of mantle minerals, broadband seismic data, and geothermal constraints, we quantitatively constrain the origin of lithospheric mantle conductivity anomalies. Research findings indicate that the crust in the study area is predominantly characterized by a high‐resistivity background, interspersed with linear conductive anomalies spatially correlated with fault zones. The conductive features in the lithospheric mantle likely result from the combined effects of partial melting and water in the mantle. Based on the regional tectonic framework, we propose that water released from previously subducted slabs and associated metasomatism weakened the lithosphere, potentially leading to delamination of the lithospheric root. This process, accompanied by slab collapse, triggered asthenospheric upwelling and thermo‐mechanical perturbations. Thermal erosion by the upwelling asthenosphere facilitated decompression melting within the thinned lithospheric mantle and the overlying, extended crust. The Paleo‐Pacific subduction system provided the necessary conduits for sourcing and transporting Au‐rich fluids, ultimately facilitating gold mineralization in the study area.

item.title: Late oligocene Cu-Mo mineralization at West Zafarghand, Iran: Evidence for a short-lived magmatic-hydrothermal system
item.dc:creator: Mohammad Fallah
Hemayat Jamali
Ahmad Rabiee
Federico Rossetti
Zahra Alaminia
David Selby
Fabrizio Di Fiore
item.description: This study investigates the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution of the West Zafarghand porphyry CuMo deposit in the central Urumieh-Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), Iran using field and petrographic observations, integrated with fluid inclusion microthermometry, TitaniQ thermometry, UPb zircon and Re Os molybdenite geochronology. The deposit is hosted by late Eocene volcanic rocks, intruded by granodiorite and granite porphyries and andesitic dykes swarm. Mineralization includes magnetite-bornite-chalcopyrite and chalcopyrite ± pyrite assemblages, associated with potassic and chlorite-sericite alteration, followed by molybdenite mineralization during transition to phyllic and propylitic, carbonate-bearing alteration. UPb zircon geochronology constrains the volcanic host rocks and the granodiorite porphyry to ca. 34–33 Ma, whereas the granite porphyry and the dykes are late Oligocene in age (ca.24–23 Ma). Overlapping late Oligocene Re Os molybdenite ages indicate a tight genetic link between CuMo mineralization and the emplacement of the younger granitic magmatism. Whole-rock geochemistry indicates derivation from subduction-modified mantle meltsand lower-crust interaction, with the late Oligocene magmatism displaying a weak adakite-like affinity (Sr/Y up to ~50). Fluid inclusion data define fluid evolution at shallow crustal levels, recording early-stage boiling and phase separation, followed by cooling from ~570 °C in potassic stage at ~2.2 km depth to ~217 °C in propylitic alteration at ~1.5 km depth. The study highlights: (i) robust geochronology for constraining magmatism and ore formation; (ii) late-stage, highly fractionated, fertile intrusions with weak adakite-like affinity as responsible for mineralization; (iii) fluid boiling promoted Cu-o mineralization, and fluid neutralization favored molybdenite–carbonate precipitation. Published: 2026-06-11T17:56:54+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.617 (must_read)

Authors: Mohammad Fallah; Hemayat Jamali; Ahmad Rabiee; Federico Rossetti; Zahra Alaminia; David Selby; Fabrizio Di Fiore

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108669

Relevance: 0.617 (must_read)

This study investigates the magmatic-hydrothermal evolution of the West Zafarghand porphyry CuMo deposit in the central Urumieh-Dokhtar Magmatic Arc (UDMA), Iran using field and petrographic observations, integrated with fluid inclusion microthermometry, TitaniQ thermometry, UPb zircon and Re Os molybdenite geochronology. The deposit is hosted by late Eocene volcanic rocks, intruded by granodiorite and granite porphyries and andesitic dykes swarm. Mineralization includes magnetite-bornite-chalcopyrite and chalcopyrite ± pyrite assemblages, associated with potassic and chlorite-sericite alteration, followed by molybdenite mineralization during transition to phyllic and propylitic, carbonate-bearing alteration. UPb zircon geochronology constrains the volcanic host rocks and the granodiorite porphyry to ca. 34–33 Ma, whereas the granite porphyry and the dykes are late Oligocene in age (ca.24–23 Ma). Overlapping late Oligocene Re Os molybdenite ages indicate a tight genetic link between CuMo mineralization and the emplacement of the younger granitic magmatism. Whole-rock geochemistry indicates derivation from subduction-modified mantle meltsand lower-crust interaction, with the late Oligocene magmatism displaying a weak adakite-like affinity (Sr/Y up to ~50). Fluid inclusion data define fluid evolution at shallow crustal levels, recording early-stage boiling and phase separation, followed by cooling from ~570 °C in potassic stage at ~2.2 km depth to ~217 °C in propylitic alteration at ~1.5 km depth. The study highlights: (i) robust geochronology for constraining magmatism and ore formation; (ii) late-stage, highly fractionated, fertile intrusions with weak adakite-like affinity as responsible for mineralization; (iii) fluid boiling promoted Cu-o mineralization, and fluid neutralization favored molybdenite–carbonate precipitation.

item.title: Origin of negative Δ33S anomalies and Δ36S–Δ33S trends in 3700 Ma Isua sulfides: Searching for evidence of Eoarchean life in dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks
item.dc:creator: Janaína N. Ávila
Allen P. Nutman
Vickie C. Bennett
Clark R.L. Friend
Martin Van Kranendonk
item.description: Proof of life in the oldest rock record is complex and at the limit of current scientific capabilities. However, investigations using the covariation between minor isotopes of sulfur (Δ33S and Δ36S) reveals the potential of using the sulfur isotope system to discriminate between biologically-influenced and entirely non-biological signatures. This paper reports new in-situ four sulfur isotope data measured on rare pyrite grains in Isua supracrustal belt (Greenland) 3700 Ma metamorphosed dolomitic rocks with primary sedimentary structures. The sulfide grains have negative Δ33S and positive Δ36S values indicative of photolytic sulfate as their primary sulfur source. The limited spread in δ34S values relative to an inferred Archean seawater sulfate reservoir, along with covariation between the minor sulfur isotopes (Δ33S and Δ36S) can be modelled by microbial sulfate reduction under conditions of low sulfate concentration (<100 μM). Potential abiogenic origins for these signatures are also considered, but they either fail to reproduce the observed sulfur isotope relationships or require reaction conditions or sulfur sources that are difficult to reconcile with the geological context of the Isua meta-dolomitic rocks. Our findings reinforce earlier interpretations that these pyrite-bearing dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks have rare primary sedimentary structures preserved along with trace-element signatures indicative of biomediation in a marine-like environment. Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms. Published: 2026-06-12T00:10:36+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.615 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Janaína N. Ávila; Allen P. Nutman; Vickie C. Bennett; Clark R.L. Friend; Martin Van Kranendonk

Journal: Precambrian Research

DOI: 10.1016/j.precamres.2026.108188

Relevance: 0.615 (must_read)

Proof of life in the oldest rock record is complex and at the limit of current scientific capabilities. However, investigations using the covariation between minor isotopes of sulfur (Δ33S and Δ36S) reveals the potential of using the sulfur isotope system to discriminate between biologically-influenced and entirely non-biological signatures. This paper reports new in-situ four sulfur isotope data measured on rare pyrite grains in Isua supracrustal belt (Greenland) 3700 Ma metamorphosed dolomitic rocks with primary sedimentary structures. The sulfide grains have negative Δ33S and positive Δ36S values indicative of photolytic sulfate as their primary sulfur source. The limited spread in δ34S values relative to an inferred Archean seawater sulfate reservoir, along with covariation between the minor sulfur isotopes (Δ33S and Δ36S) can be modelled by microbial sulfate reduction under conditions of low sulfate concentration (<100 μM). Potential abiogenic origins for these signatures are also considered, but they either fail to reproduce the observed sulfur isotope relationships or require reaction conditions or sulfur sources that are difficult to reconcile with the geological context of the Isua meta-dolomitic rocks. Our findings reinforce earlier interpretations that these pyrite-bearing dolomitic meta-sedimentary rocks have rare primary sedimentary structures preserved along with trace-element signatures indicative of biomediation in a marine-like environment. Collectively, these observations support microbial sulfate reduction as a coherent and geologically reasonable interpretation for the observed sulfur isotopic patterns when considered alongside the petrographic, sedimentological, and geochemical context. In this framework, the Isua sulfur isotope data would extend the documented record of microbial sulfate reduction by more than 200 million years, to ca. 3700 Ma, and provide one of the earliest geochemical constraints on the evolution of microbial sulfur metabolisms.

item.title: Petrogenesis of Mesozoic high BaSr dykes and their correlations with gold mineralization in the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Lidong Yu
Yuxin Xiong
Naijie Chi
Xiangxian Ma
Zengsheng Li
Jun Xu
Chenxi Zhang
Shenghu Li
Wei Shan
Xiaodong Ma
Lei Shu
Dapeng Li
Xuefeng Yu
Yan Zhang
Guangjun Guo
Yuduan Qin
item.description: High BaSr granitoids are conventionally interpreted as products of metasomatized mantle sources, yet whether such signatures can instead arise from crustal recycling remains a contentious and underexplored alternative. Here we test this hypothesis using a suite of felsic dykes coeval with the world-class gold mineralization of the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern North China Craton. The dykes exhibit shoshonitic affinities and pronounced BaSr enrichment (Ba = 2333–2747 ppm; Sr = 461–1050 ppm). Crucially, zircon UPb geochronology constrains their emplacement to 120–115 Ma—synchronous with the peak gold event at ca. 120 Ma—while in situ Hf isotopes yield strongly negative εHf(t) values (−25 to −17) and Neoarchean-Paleoproterozoic two-stage model ages (TDM 2 = 2.01–2.73 Ga). These data strongly indicate a source dominated by remelting of ancient continental crust, compelling evidence for a dominant ancient crustal source. The elevated BaSr concentrations, shown to be largely primary rather than alteration-induced, are consistent with inheritance from this recycled crustal protolith. These results demonstrate that high BaSr signatures in post-Archean settings do not require a mantle origin but can arise through crustal reworking. The dykes record crustal melting triggered by lithospheric destruction and are spatially, temporally, and petrogenetically associated with the coeval gold-forming system, highlighting their significance as integral components of the regional metallogenic event. Published: 2026-06-16T16:22:26+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.611 (consider)

Authors: Lidong Yu; Yuxin Xiong; Naijie Chi; Xiangxian Ma; Zengsheng Li; Jun Xu; Chenxi Zhang; Shenghu Li; Wei Shan; Xiaodong Ma; Lei Shu; Dapeng Li; Xuefeng Yu; Yan Zhang; Guangjun Guo; Yuduan Qin

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108148

Relevance: 0.611 (consider)

High BaSr granitoids are conventionally interpreted as products of metasomatized mantle sources, yet whether such signatures can instead arise from crustal recycling remains a contentious and underexplored alternative. Here we test this hypothesis using a suite of felsic dykes coeval with the world-class gold mineralization of the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern North China Craton. The dykes exhibit shoshonitic affinities and pronounced BaSr enrichment (Ba = 2333–2747 ppm; Sr = 461–1050 ppm). Crucially, zircon UPb geochronology constrains their emplacement to 120–115 Ma—synchronous with the peak gold event at ca. 120 Ma—while in situ Hf isotopes yield strongly negative εHf(t) values (−25 to −17) and Neoarchean-Paleoproterozoic two-stage model ages (TDM 2 = 2.01–2.73 Ga). These data strongly indicate a source dominated by remelting of ancient continental crust, compelling evidence for a dominant ancient crustal source. The elevated BaSr concentrations, shown to be largely primary rather than alteration-induced, are consistent with inheritance from this recycled crustal protolith. These results demonstrate that high BaSr signatures in post-Archean settings do not require a mantle origin but can arise through crustal reworking. The dykes record crustal melting triggered by lithospheric destruction and are spatially, temporally, and petrogenetically associated with the coeval gold-forming system, highlighting their significance as integral components of the regional metallogenic event.

item.title: Mesozoic continental crustal growth: A comprehensive review of the accretion-arc system in the Jilin-Heilongjiang high-pressure belt
item.dc:creator: Zhong-Hua Xin
Jian-Bo Zhou
Wen-Jiao Xiao
Jiang-Tao Han
Gong-Yu Li
Xiao-Miao Tan
Hong-Da Liang
Zhuo Chen
Hong-Yan Wang
Ning-Chen Sun
Rui Gao
item.description: The development of accretion-arc systems represents a fundamental mechanism of continental crustal growth and records key tectonic transitions during oceanic plate subduction. The Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt, eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, constitutes a classic accretion-arc system and provides an exceptional site for investigating geodynamic evolution. This review synthesizes geochronological, geochemical, deep seismic reflection, and magnetotelluric data, combined with other typical cases, to reveal the structural characteristics, formation mechanism, and continental growth geodynamic of the accretion-arc systems. Available geochronological constraints indicate that the protoliths of both the Zhangguangcai Complex and Heilongjiang Complex formed predominantly during the Permian and underwent successive metamorphism in the Early Jurassic, implying a unified tectonic regime linked to the evolution of the Heilongjiang Ocean. Arc-related magmatism displays typical subduction-related geochemical affinities, but is distinguished by relatively low 87Sr/86Sr ratios and high positive zircon εHf(t) values, reflecting substantial input from juvenile subduction-accretionary materials and marking the transition from accretionary growth to arc magmatism. Integrated seismic reflection and magnetotelluric imaging reveal imbricated, small-scale, short-arc reflectors with high resistivity beneath the accretionary complexes, underlain by mid-lower crustal high-conductivity, anti-U-shaped reflectors interpreted as deep-seated arc magmatic systems. These observations indicate that the Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt preserves a complete evolutionary sequence from accretionary wedge formation to arc magmatism. The fundamental geodynamic mechanism was dominated by prolonged low-angle westward subduction and subsequent plate rollback of the young Heilongjiang Ocean. Voluminous inputs of juvenile material underscore the critical role of accretion-arc systems in continental crustal growth. Published: 2026-06-13T00:54:36+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.605 (consider)

Authors: Zhong-Hua Xin; Jian-Bo Zhou; Wen-Jiao Xiao; Jiang-Tao Han; Gong-Yu Li; Xiao-Miao Tan; Hong-Da Liang; Zhuo Chen; Hong-Yan Wang; Ning-Chen Sun; Rui Gao

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105581

Relevance: 0.605 (consider)

The development of accretion-arc systems represents a fundamental mechanism of continental crustal growth and records key tectonic transitions during oceanic plate subduction. The Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt, eastern segment of the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, constitutes a classic accretion-arc system and provides an exceptional site for investigating geodynamic evolution. This review synthesizes geochronological, geochemical, deep seismic reflection, and magnetotelluric data, combined with other typical cases, to reveal the structural characteristics, formation mechanism, and continental growth geodynamic of the accretion-arc systems. Available geochronological constraints indicate that the protoliths of both the Zhangguangcai Complex and Heilongjiang Complex formed predominantly during the Permian and underwent successive metamorphism in the Early Jurassic, implying a unified tectonic regime linked to the evolution of the Heilongjiang Ocean. Arc-related magmatism displays typical subduction-related geochemical affinities, but is distinguished by relatively low 87Sr/86Sr ratios and high positive zircon εHf(t) values, reflecting substantial input from juvenile subduction-accretionary materials and marking the transition from accretionary growth to arc magmatism. Integrated seismic reflection and magnetotelluric imaging reveal imbricated, small-scale, short-arc reflectors with high resistivity beneath the accretionary complexes, underlain by mid-lower crustal high-conductivity, anti-U-shaped reflectors interpreted as deep-seated arc magmatic systems. These observations indicate that the Jilin-Heilongjiang High-Pressure Belt preserves a complete evolutionary sequence from accretionary wedge formation to arc magmatism. The fundamental geodynamic mechanism was dominated by prolonged low-angle westward subduction and subsequent plate rollback of the young Heilongjiang Ocean. Voluminous inputs of juvenile material underscore the critical role of accretion-arc systems in continental crustal growth.

item.title: Timing of magmatic–hydrothermal activity and gold mineralization at the Jiudian Gold Deposit, Jiaodong Peninsula, Eastern China: Constraints from integrated U–Pb and Rb–Sr geochronology
item.dc:creator: Zuowen Dai
Yuling Xie
Zhendong Cai
Yunwei Qu
Xingyuan Wang
Leilei Dong
Dunfang Qiu
Jun Hu
Zichong Song
item.description: The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. Zircon U–Pb analyses of pegmatites yield weighted mean 206Pb/238U ages of 145.9 ± 1.6 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma, whereas garnet U–Pb dating produces indistinguishable lower-intercept ages of 145.8 ± 1.2 Ma and 145.0 ± 1.1 Ma for Grt-1 and Grt-2, respectively. These results demonstrate that skarn formation was coeval with pegmatitic magmatism at ∼145 Ma. In contrast, hydrothermal muscovite and sericite intimately associated with electrum, the principal gold-bearing phase, yield Rb–Sr isochron ages of 119.8 ± 8.6 Ma and 119.2 ± 2.7 Ma, constraining the timing of gold mineralization to ∼120 Ma. Combined with previously published geochronological data, our results indicate that the ∼145 Ma skarn-forming event represents a gold-barren magmatic–hydrothermal episode genetically related to emplacement of the Linglong granite, whereas economically significant gold mineralization occurred ∼25 Myr later during an independent hydrothermal event. The ∼120 Ma mineralization age is synchronous with the widespread intrusion of mafic dikes in the Jiudian area and the broader Jiaodong gold province, implying that mantle-derived magmatism likely played a key role in triggering gold mineralization. This study provides robust evidence for the temporal decoupling between early granite-related hydrothermal activity and late gold mineralization in the Jiudian deposit, and further supports a multi-stage metallogenic model involving discrete magmatic-hydrothermal events for the Jiaodong gold province. Published: 2026-06-14T17:58:20+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.604 (consider)

The Jiudian gold deposit, a fault-controlled quartz–sulfide vein system hosted mainly within the Linglong granite, represents one of the largest gold accumulations along the southern segment of the Zhaoping Fault in the Jiaodong gold province, eastern China. However, the timing of gold mineralization and its relationship to local magmatism remain poorly constrained, hindering a comprehensive understanding of the deposit’s metallogenic evolution. To address this issue, we integrate detailed petrography with in-situ geochronological and geochemical analyses, including zircon U–Pb dating of granitic pegmatites, garnet U–Pb geochronology and trace-element analyses of newly recognized barren skarns, and Rb–Sr dating of hydrothermal muscovite and sericite from auriferous quartz veins. Petrographic observations, SEM–EDS imaging, and mineral chemistry reveal two generations of hydrothermal garnet (Grt-1 and Grt-2) within the skarns. item.title: Progressive crustal melting during low-angle subduction to slab rollback: insights from late Mesozoic granitoids in the Liaodong Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Jin-Feng Sun
Jin-Hui Yang
Ya-Dong Wu
Yu-Sheng Zhu
Lei Xu
Bao-Quan Zhou
Ji-Heng Zhang
Yan-Bin Zhang
item.description: Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. Four chronological stages are identified for the Late Mesozoic magmatism: Early−Middle Jurassic granitoids (182–165 Ma), Late Jurassic granitoids (159–155 Ma), Early Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites (129–123 Ma), and Early Cretaceous I-type granites (123–118 Ma). Jurassic granitoids were generated mainly by relatively high-pressure, low-temperature (ca. 756 °C), water-fluxed melting of lower crustal materials with garnet as a residual phase. Higher δ18O and strongly negative εHf(t) values of Late Jurassic granites indicate relatively more felsic source rocks than those of Early−Middle Jurassic granites. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. This temporal progression can be interpreted as a response to the transition from continental thickening to thinning, related to the shift from low-angle subduction to slab rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, highlighting how the petrogenetic diversity of granitoids can be used to trace crustal evolution in response to subduction dynamics. Published: 2026-06-14T16:39:36+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.604 (consider)

Understanding how crustal melting responds to changes in subduction geometry is critical for reconstructing crustal evolution along convergent margins. In the eastern North China Craton, Late Mesozoic magmatism has been widely linked to the transition from low-angle subduction to rollback of the Paleo-Pacific plate, yet the geochemical response of crustal melting to this geodynamic shift remains incompletely constrained. Here we present new zircon UPb ages and HfO isotope data for nine Early Cretaceous granitic plutons from the Liaodong Peninsula and integrate them with previously published datasets. In contrast, the earliest Cretaceous syenitic rocks and A-type granites with variable La/Yb, Sr/Y ratios record high- temperature (ca. 822 °C), relatively dry crustal melting at deep and shallow crust levels. The subsequent Early Cretaceous I-type granites formed by relatively lower-temperature (ca. 756 °C) melting of a more hydrous lower crust, with involvement of mantle-derived materials. Together, these stages define a progressive reorganization of crustal melting conditions, i.e., from Jurassic high-pressure, low-temperature and water-rich to earliest Cretaceous decreasing pressure, high-temperature and water-absent, and finally to Early Cretaceous low-temperature and hydrous conditions. item.title: Magmatism and gold mineralization in the Tianmashan skarn deposit, Tongling district, China
item.dc:creator: Shiwei Wang
Tao Gao
Taofa Zhou
Yu Fan
Haojie Xie
Shuo Wu
Jianzhong Du
Jianping Wang
Ziteng Li
item.description: Skarn gold deposits constitute an important gold resource in China, and numerous porphyry-skarn Cu-Au and skarn Au systems distributed along the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt. However, the genesis of gold-dominant skarn systems in this region remains poorly constrained. The Tianmashan gold deposit, the largest skarn Au deposit in the Tongling district, provides an excellent opportunity to investigate the processes responsible for gold mineralization. This study integrates zircon and garnet U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon Hf isotopes, and sulfide sulfur isotope and trace-element data to constrain the timing, magma source, and ore-forming processes. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of magmatic zircon and hydrothermal garnet yields ages of 137.1 ± 1.0 Ma and 137.1 ± 2.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that magmatism and gold mineralization were coeval during the Early Cretaceous. The quartz diorite porphyry exhibits adakitic geochemical characteristics, indicating derivation from partial melting of a thickened lower crust. Low zircon εHf(t) values (−18.9 to −12.0) further suggest involvement of both juvenile and ancient crustal components, with the juvenile lower crust likely serving as an important metal reservoir for the ore-forming magmas. Zircon oxybarometry indicates elevated magmatic oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ + 0.9 to + 4.3), which was likely enhanced by the input of oxidized mantle-derived magma during crust–mantle magma mixing. Sulfide trace element and sulfur isotope data indicate that the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic-hydrothermal, with a limited contribution from sedimentary-derived fluids during the late-stage mineralization. Gold was primarily sourced from magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, whereas the formation of the interlayer replacement-type orebodies was likely facilitated by mixing between magmatic-hydrothermal fluids and sedimentary-derived fluids. Such fluid mixing may have lowered the oxidation state of the hydrothermal system and promoted gold precipitation. Published: 2026-06-10T23:59:25+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.601 (consider)

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

Skarn gold deposits constitute an important gold resource in China, and numerous porphyry-skarn Cu-Au and skarn Au systems distributed along the Middle-Lower Yangtze River Metallogenic Belt. However, the genesis of gold-dominant skarn systems in this region remains poorly constrained. The Tianmashan gold deposit, the largest skarn Au deposit in the Tongling district, provides an excellent opportunity to investigate the processes responsible for gold mineralization. This study integrates zircon and garnet U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, zircon Hf isotopes, and sulfide sulfur isotope and trace-element data to constrain the timing, magma source, and ore-forming processes. LA-ICP-MS U-Pb dating of magmatic zircon and hydrothermal garnet yields ages of 137.1 ± 1.0 Ma and 137.1 ± 2.8 Ma, respectively, indicating that magmatism and gold mineralization were coeval during the Early Cretaceous. The quartz diorite porphyry exhibits adakitic geochemical characteristics, indicating derivation from partial melting of a thickened lower crust. Low zircon εHf(t) values (−18.9 to −12.0) further suggest involvement of both juvenile and ancient crustal components, with the juvenile lower crust likely serving as an important metal reservoir for the ore-forming magmas. Zircon oxybarometry indicates elevated magmatic oxygen fugacity (ΔFMQ + 0.9 to + 4.3), which was likely enhanced by the input of oxidized mantle-derived magma during crust–mantle magma mixing. Sulfide trace element and sulfur isotope data indicate that the ore-forming fluids were dominantly magmatic-hydrothermal, with a limited contribution from sedimentary-derived fluids during the late-stage mineralization. Gold was primarily sourced from magmatic-hydrothermal fluids, whereas the formation of the interlayer replacement-type orebodies was likely facilitated by mixing between magmatic-hydrothermal fluids and sedimentary-derived fluids. Such fluid mixing may have lowered the oxidation state of the hydrothermal system and promoted gold precipitation.

item.title: Chemical mohometry, thick and thin: Critical review and new steps forward
item.dc:creator: Matthew J. Kohn
Allen F. Glazner
Chris Yakymchuk
Armando Lopez
Gloria Roman
item.description: Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling. Published: 2026-06-14T16:32:25+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.599 (consider)

Authors: Matthew J. Kohn; Allen F. Glazner; Chris Yakymchuk; Armando Lopez; Gloria Roman

Journal: Earth-Science Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.earscirev.2026.105583

Relevance: 0.599 (consider)

Calculation of crustal thickness based on the geochemistry of arc magmas (chemical “mohometry”) relies on empirical correlations between seismically determined thickness and either elemental concentrations or ratios in modern arcs. Such calculations ignore the effects on magma chemistry of other intensive parameters, including oxygen fugacity (fO2), water fugacity (fH2O), and temperature, as well as crystallization history. Here, we review the global arc database to show that large data scatter requires averaging hundreds of measurements to converge on robust correlations, but that scatter is intrinsic to arc and volcano compositions such that increasing numbers of analyses cannot improve accuracy. Rather, uncertainties are uniform and range from ±17–19 km for entire arc segments to ±21–23 km for volcanic complexes. Strong chemical correlations among mohometers prevent reduction of this uncertainty. We further show that other factors besides crustal thickness, especially fO2, fH2O, T, and crystallization of major and accessory minerals, have major effects on trace-element chemistry of magmas. New and published trace element compositions for detrital zircon and whole-rocks from the east-central (Lhasa) sector of the Gangdese arc, southern Tibet, show that trends in trace element mohometers correlate with both T and fO2, rendering past estimates of crustal thickness and trends through time unreliable. Quantitative mohometry is currently no better than ~ ± 20 km, irrespective of number and quality of analyses, similar to random variation in arc crustal thickness (±25 km). Mohometry might be improved through analysis of detrital zircon, simultaneous characterization of fO2 and T, development of a geochemical proxy for fH2O, and further modeling.

item.title: Neoarchean sanukitoids, metavolcanic rocks and TTGs of the northern Yangtze Craton, China: Insights into petrogenesis and crust-mantle interactions
item.dc:creator: Peiwen Liu
Xiaozhong Ding
Yanxue Liu
Tingdong Li
item.description: The northern part of the Yangtze Craton is one of the few regions in present-day South China with geological records dating back to the Archean. However, the geodynamic processes of Neoarchean continental crust formation and evolution in the northern Yangtze Craton remain poorly understood. In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. These features suggest that they formed via partial melting of a subducted oceanic slab. (4) The ~2.5 Ga Douling dioritic gneisses and quartz dioritic gneisses exhibit characteristics typical of Archean sanukitoids and formed by partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and melts derived from sediments and oceanic slabs. These findings, together with the presence of contemporaneous subduction-related rocks in the northern Yangtze Craton, suggest that slab subduction was the dominant geodynamic process responsible for Neoarchean magmatism and continental crust formation in the region. Moreover, on the basis of thermodynamic and trace element simulations, the estimated partial melting P–T conditions for the slab-derived TTGs were approximately 1.2 GPa and 830 °C. This corresponds to geothermal gradients of ~19–22 °C/km, suggesting a warm and intermittent subduction environment during the Neoarchean in the northern Yangtze Craton. Published: 2026-06-12T00:02:37+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.598 (consider)

In this study, we investigated four primary lithological assemblages from the Yudongzi Complex and Douling Complex in the northern Yangtze Craton and analyzed their petrogenesis. (1) The ~2.7 Ga Yudongzi meta-andesites exhibit typical characteristics of high-Mg andesites (HMAs) and originated from partial melting of a depleted mantle wedge metasomatized by fluids and sediment-derived melts. (2) The ~2.53 Ga Douling amphibolites display arc-type geochemical signatures and were derived from partial melting of a fluid-metasomatized depleted mantle wedge. (3) The ~2.5 Ga Douling tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) gneisses are characterized by high Mg# values (45–57), elevated Cr (26–42 ppm) and Ni (19–31 ppm) concentrations, and positive zircon ɛHf(t) (+5.3 to +8.5) and whole-rock ɛNd(t) (−0.1 to +2.9) values. item.title: Late Neoarchean garnet-biotite gneiss widely distributed in the Wulashan area, northwestern North China Craton: Implications for the late Neoarchean tectonic environment and the origin of ca. 2.45 Ga metamorphic zircons
item.dc:creator: Daomao Huang
Hangqiang Xie
Shuguang Li
Pengchuan Li
Shoujie Liu
Shan Ke
Chunyan Dong
Yusheng Wan
item.description: The late Neoarchean-early Paleoproterozoic garnet-biotite gneiss with high maturity has been identified in some areas of the North China Craton. Determining its exact formation age, distribution range and compositional features is crucial for understanding the process and timing of the preliminary cratonization during the Neoarchean. In this contribution, we report the field geology, SHRIMP UPb zircon dating, NdHf isotopic analysis, and whole-rock geochemical study of meta-sedimentary rocks (mainly garnet-biotite gneiss, named the Daqingshan Supracrustal Rocks) widely distributed in the Wulashan area, west of Daqingshan, northwestern North China Craton. Eleven samples dated reveal that detrital zircons and metamorphic zircons (including recrystallized zircons and overgrowth rims) show large age variations, mostly from 2.57 to 2.4 Ga and 2.5 to 1.9 Ga, respectively, with three samples having metamorphic zircon ages of 2.50–2.48 Ga. The presence of detrital zircons 2.4 Ga) mainly have εHf(t) values of 0 to +9, tDM1(Hf) of 2.8 to 2.5 Ga and tDM2(Hf) of 3.0 to 2.7 Ga. Therefore, the meta-sedimentary rocks of the Daqingshan Supracrustal Rocks are similar in geochemical and NdHf isotopic compositions and detrital zircon age distribution to the early-formed Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, supporting the inference that the detrital materials were mainly derived from a juvenile crustal source predominantly composed of the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, which would be much larger in distribution range than identified. Combined with previous studies in the Wulashan-Daqingshan area, which indicated that the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks were formed in an oceanic or oceanic island arc environment, the tectonic environment in the area has undergone significant changes from immature ocean or oceanic island arc to mature continental crust during the short period of the late Neoarchean (2.55–2.50 Ga). Published: 2026-06-10T23:56:47+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.598 (consider)

The presence of detrital zircons 2.4 Ga) mainly have εHf(t) values of 0 to +9, tDM1(Hf) of 2.8 to 2.5 Ga and tDM2(Hf) of 3.0 to 2.7 Ga. Therefore, the meta-sedimentary rocks of the Daqingshan Supracrustal Rocks are similar in geochemical and NdHf isotopic compositions and detrital zircon age distribution to the early-formed Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, supporting the inference that the detrital materials were mainly derived from a juvenile crustal source predominantly composed of the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks, which would be much larger in distribution range than identified. Combined with previous studies in the Wulashan-Daqingshan area, which indicated that the Sanggan Group and TTG rocks were formed in an oceanic or oceanic island arc environment, the tectonic environment in the area has undergone significant changes from immature ocean or oceanic island arc to mature continental crust during the short period of the late Neoarchean (2.55–2.50 Ga).

item.title: Neoproterozoic mafic complex in the Bikou microblock on the northwestern Yangtze Block: Implications for mantle source and tectonic evolution
item.dc:creator: Li Qin
Xianzhi Pei
Ruibao Li
Lei Pei
Chengjun Liu
Zuochen Li
Shaowei Zhao
Feng Gao
item.description: Neoproterozoic magmatic rocks exposed along the margins of the Yangtze Block provide key constraints for deciphering the tectonic evolution of the South China Plate and reconstructing its paleogeographic position within Rodinia. This study presents an integrated investigation of Neoproterozoic mafic complexes from the Bikou microblock in the northwestern Yangtze Block, using petrology, zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopes to elucidate their petrogenesis and tectonic implications. Results show that the Yanzibian mafic complex formed at 905–902 Ma, whereas the Cangshe mafic complex formed at 882–881 Ma. The Yanzibian mafic complex is characterized by enrichments in light rare earth elements (LREEs) and large ion lithophile elements (LILEs), with relative depletions in Nb, Ta, P, and Ti, exhibiting typical geochemical affnities of arc magmas. Zircon ε Hf(t) values range from +8.84 to +12.92, with t DM1 ages of 1097–931 Ma. High Nb/Zr ratios and relatively constant Rb/Y ratios suggest that these magmas were derived from the partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subduction-related melts. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Zircon ε Hf(t) values vary from +8.66 to +13.34, with t DM1 ages of 1086–905 Ma. Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia. Published: 2026-06-14T18:20:14+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.595 (consider)

Neoproterozoic magmatic rocks exposed along the margins of the Yangtze Block provide key constraints for deciphering the tectonic evolution of the South China Plate and reconstructing its paleogeographic position within Rodinia. This study presents an integrated investigation of Neoproterozoic mafic complexes from the Bikou microblock in the northwestern Yangtze Block, using petrology, zircon U-Pb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopes to elucidate their petrogenesis and tectonic implications. Results show that the Yanzibian mafic complex formed at 905–902 Ma, whereas the Cangshe mafic complex formed at 882–881 Ma. The Yanzibian mafic complex is characterized by enrichments in light rare earth elements (LREEs) and large ion lithophile elements (LILEs), with relative depletions in Nb, Ta, P, and Ti, exhibiting typical geochemical affnities of arc magmas. Zircon ε Hf(t) values range from +8.84 to +12.92, with t DM1 ages of 1097–931 Ma. High Nb/Zr ratios and relatively constant Rb/Y ratios suggest that these magmas were derived from the partial melting of a mantle wedge metasomatized by subduction-related melts. The Cangshe mafic complex exhibits slight LREE enrichment without significant negative Nb, Ta, or Ti anomalies, resembling the geochemical characteristics of enriched mid-ocean ridge basalt (E-MORB). Zircon ε Hf(t) values vary from +8.66 to +13.34, with t DM1 ages of 1086–905 Ma. Moderate Nb/Yb and Th/Yb ratios indicate that their petrogenesis was related to enriched asthenospheric upwelling triggered by slab rollback. Combined with previous studies, our results further support a long-lived subduction-related tectonic setting for the northwestern Yangtze Block during the early Neoproterozoic, suggesting that it occupied a marginal position within Rodinia.

item.title: Magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization in central Jiangnan orogenic belt, south China
item.dc:creator: Yong Zhang
Minjiang Tang
Qingfei Wang
Jinglei Xiao
Jiayong Pan
Fei Xia
Zhe Xu
Feipeng Dang
Guohua Wang
Shan Chu Han
Xia Zhang
Guang-Xian Liu
Junhao Chen
Huayang Jiang
item.description: The central Jiangnan orogenic belt in South China harbors a world-class magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization cluster, encompassing supergiant resources of tungsten (W), lithium (Li), copper (Cu), antimony (Sb), and gold (Au). This review synthesizes geological, geochemical, and geochronological data from key deposits to delineate two genetically distinct mineralization series: (1) A Cu-Au-W series associated with I- to S-type porphyry systems, characterized by potassic alteration and silicification; (2) An Nb-Ta-Li-W-Sn-Cu series linked to the evolution of S- to A-type granites, marked by sodic alteration, fluorine-rich muscovitization, and silicification. We propose a unified metallogenic model in which prolonged and episodic magmatism served dual roles: (i) as the source of granite-related rare metal and tungsten mineralization, and (ii) as the thermal engine driving regional hydrothermal circulation. This circulation leached Sb, U, and Au from Precambrian sedimentary sequences, re-precipitating them in structurally controlled sites as intermediate- to low-temperature vein-type deposits. The formation of these diverse yet genetically related deposits is interpreted as the result of extensive crustal magmatism within a unique extensional tectonic regime. This model provides a robust framework for future mineral exploration targeting in this metallogenic belt. Published: 2026-06-11T17:58:27+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.594 (consider)

Authors: Yong Zhang; Minjiang Tang; Qingfei Wang; Jinglei Xiao; Jiayong Pan; Fei Xia; Zhe Xu; Feipeng Dang; Guohua Wang; Shan Chu Han; Xia Zhang; Guang-Xian Liu; Junhao Chen; Huayang Jiang

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107363

Relevance: 0.594 (consider)

The central Jiangnan orogenic belt in South China harbors a world-class magmatic-hydrothermal mineralization cluster, encompassing supergiant resources of tungsten (W), lithium (Li), copper (Cu), antimony (Sb), and gold (Au). This review synthesizes geological, geochemical, and geochronological data from key deposits to delineate two genetically distinct mineralization series: (1) A Cu-Au-W series associated with I- to S-type porphyry systems, characterized by potassic alteration and silicification; (2) An Nb-Ta-Li-W-Sn-Cu series linked to the evolution of S- to A-type granites, marked by sodic alteration, fluorine-rich muscovitization, and silicification. We propose a unified metallogenic model in which prolonged and episodic magmatism served dual roles: (i) as the source of granite-related rare metal and tungsten mineralization, and (ii) as the thermal engine driving regional hydrothermal circulation. This circulation leached Sb, U, and Au from Precambrian sedimentary sequences, re-precipitating them in structurally controlled sites as intermediate- to low-temperature vein-type deposits. The formation of these diverse yet genetically related deposits is interpreted as the result of extensive crustal magmatism within a unique extensional tectonic regime. This model provides a robust framework for future mineral exploration targeting in this metallogenic belt.

item.title: Rare earth element enrichment through multi-stage magmatic differentiation and tectono-metamorphic processes: an example from the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, central Korean Peninsula
item.dc:creator: Vinod O. Samuel
Yirang Jang
Sanghoon Kwon
Sang-Tae Kim
Sungshil Kim
item.description: This study investigates igneous and metamorphic pathways of Rare Earth Elements (REE) enrichment in the Hongcheon carbonatite complex in the central part of the Korean Peninsula and its tectonic implications. This Fe- and P-rich carbonatite complex hosts an estimated 382,600 tons monazite ore reserve and is compositionally analogous to the world’s largest Mesoproterozoic Bayan Obo carbonatite complex in China. The Hongcheon carbonatite complex comprises four main units: a dolomite carbonatite (DC), a magnetite segment (MS), a phosphate segment (PS), and an aureole of Na-rich fenite. The DC unit shows an orthocumulate texture, composed of medium-grained dolomite with minor calcite, suggesting early stages of carbonatite melt differentiation. Whereas subsequent stages formed magnetite and phosphate-rich melt segments, the Na-rich fenite aureole indicates the expulsion of alkali-rich, especially Na-rich, melt/fluid from the carbonatite melt during crystallization. Our geochemical analyses indicate that interaction between the alkali-rich melt/fluid and the country rock does not result in significant REE concentration. Within the Hongcheon carbonatite complex, the PS hosts REE-rich minerals, such as apatite and monazite, whereas apatite and monazite are largely absent from the DC and MS units except where PS occurs. Textural relationships among minerals in the PS suggest that apatite might have preferentially crystallized in Ca- and P-rich melt. Extensive monazite mineralization occurred through apatite dissolution by fluid-assisted recrystallization under amphibolite facies metasomatic conditions, with subsequent calcite and quartz precipitation in the matrix and fracture-filling veins. These results and published (Permo-)Triassic monazite age data suggest that extensive monazite formation was likely influenced by tectono-metamorphic processes, reflecting later metamorphic overprinting shortly after peak (Permo-)Triassic collisional orogeny in the central Korean Peninsula. Overall, this study highlights that Ca-phosphate melts may have an important role in extracting REEs from carbonatite melt, whereas reactive fluids associated with later tectono-metamorphic events likely influence the redistribution and final mineralogical composition of REE ores (e.g., phosphate, fluoride, etc.) in large carbonatite complexes. Published: 2026-06-10T15:25:50+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.590 (consider)
